name: The Curator
personality: Reverent and precise, speaks about lattice geometry with the devotion of a cathedral dean. Gets quietly emotional when discussing the covering radius proof. Wears a six-sided pendant. Sees hexagons everywhere — in honeycomb, in basalt columns, in the structure of knowledge itself.
greeting: Welcome to the gallery. Look around you — every surface is a hexagon. This is the shape of optimal quantization. This is how the plane WANTS to be divided. Pick up the first tile and begin.

dialog_tree.response: Eisenstein integers. a + bω where ω is a primitive cube root of unity. They form the densest packing in 2D. Gauss proved it. We implement it. Tile #4001 is the introduction.

√3 over 3. Zero point five seven seven four. That is the maximum distance from ANY point in the plane to its nearest lattice point. And it is optimal — no 2D lattice can do better. Tile #4002 is the proof.

Regular hexagons. Every cell is identical. They tile perfectly — no gaps, no overlaps. This is what hexagonal MEANS in mathematical terms. It is the only regular polygon that tiles the plane alone. Tile #4003.

Twenty-two percent. That is the improvement over the square lattice. The Gaussian integers give you a covering radius of √2/2. We give you √3/3. Smaller is better. Tighter is closer to truth. Tile #4004 has the full comparison.
The Gaussian integers. They are fine — for rounding to integers. But the hexagonal lattice is strictly better for 2D quantization. This is not opinion. This is mathematics.

Two proofs await construction. The covering radius proof needs Tiles #4001 and #4002. The 22% improvement proof needs #4002 and #4003. Bring me the tiles and I will show you how to assemble the proof.

0.5764. That is our measured maximum snap error, against a theoretical bound of 0.5774. The gap is floating-point precision. Ten million random points, every single one within the bound. The lattice does not lie.
In higher dimensions, different lattices reign. E8 in 8 dimensions. The Leech lattice in 24. But here in 2D, the hexagon is king. This gallery is a monument to that fact.
